title: Today's AI Brief: OpenAI's Public Policy Agenda
summary: OpenAI outlines its public policy agenda for AI, while Google showcases demos of Gemini Omni and Gemini 3.5.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI brief. Today, I'm looking at some significant developments in the AI space. OpenAI has just published its public policy agenda, outlining how it plans to ensure AI benefits society. Meanwhile, Google is showing off its Gemini models in action.

OpenAI's public policy agenda is a big deal. They're focusing on safety, youth protection, workforce transition, and global standards. This is interesting because it shows that as AI becomes more powerful, the companies behind it are thinking about how to mitigate risks and make sure these technologies are used for good.

Google has shared nine demos of its Gemini Omni and Gemini 3.5 models. These demos show how Gemini's ability to reason and create can be applied in different ways. My read is that these models are pushing the boundaries of what's possible with AI, and it's exciting to see them in action.

What stands out to me is that both of these developments are about making AI more accessible and trustworthy. OpenAI's agenda is all about ensuring AI benefits society, while Google's demos show how AI can be used in practical ways. It's a bit like the platform shifts we saw with mobile and the web - AI is changing the game, and companies are figuring out how to make it work for everyone.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.

sources.title: 9 demos of Gemini Omni and Gemini 3.5 in action
